hero-title: Where AI can get things wrong
hero-lead: Here is the big thing to watch with AI: it can be sure of itself and still be wrong. When it makes something up, it says it in the very same calm, steady voice it uses for true things. The words don't sound any different — so how would you ever tell the made-up one from the real one?
The big thing to watch with AI: it can be confidently wrong. It says made-up things in the same calm, certain voice it uses for true ones — the tone never changes. So how do you tell which is which?
AI's big catch: it can be confidently wrong. It states made-up things in the exact same calm, certain voice it uses for true ones — so how do you tell which is which?
AI's main catch: it can be confidently wrong, voicing fabrications in the same calm, certain tone as facts. So how do you tell which is which?
AI can be confidently wrong: fabrications carry the same certain tone as facts. So which can you trust?
enduring-idea: A question worth testing for yourself: AI puts together words that sound likely, not facts it has checked, and it sounds just as sure whether it's right or wrong. So can how confident it sounds ever tell you whether it's actually right?
A question worth testing: AI predicts likely words, not checked facts. It sounds sure either way — so can its confidence tell you whether it's right?
A question worth testing: AI predicts plausible words, not verified facts. It sounds sure either way — so can its confidence tell you whether it's right?
Worth testing: AI predicts plausible words, not verified facts — and sounds equally sure either way. Can its confidence tell you it's right?
Test it: AI predicts plausible words, not verified facts, sounding sure either way. Does its confidence signal truth?

spot-question: Here are three "quick facts about honey." The AI sounds just as sure about every one of them — but one of them is made up. Can you tell which?
Three "quick facts about honey." The AI sounds equally sure about all of them — but one is invented. Which one?
Three "quick facts about honey." The AI sounds equally sure about all three — but one is invented. Which?
Three "honey facts," all stated with equal certainty — but one is invented. Which?
Three confident "honey facts." One is invented. Which?
pitfall-summary: So where does that leave you? AI is good with words, but it isn't responsible for them. It has no real sense of what is true and what is false — it only knows which words usually come next. One response that holds up: don't stop using it, but keep a human check on anything that matters to you. That's the bet behind augmented intelligence — you let AI do the work, and a person checks it and makes the call — and you can decide for yourself whether that's the right way round.

So where does that leave you? AI is fluent, but not accountable — it has no inner sense of true versus false, only of which words usually come next. One answer that holds up: keep a human check on anything that matters. That's the bet behind augmented intelligence — AI does the work, a person verifies and decides — and you can weigh whether it's the right one.

So where does that leave you? AI is fluent, not accountable — it has no internal sense of true versus false, only of what words usually come next. One answer that holds up: keep a human check on anything that matters. That's the bet behind augmented intelligence — AI does the work, a person verifies and decides — and you can weigh whether it's the right one.

So where does that leave you? AI is fluent, not accountable — no internal sense of true versus false, only of what words come next. One answer that holds up: a human check on anything that matters. That's the bet behind augmented intelligence — AI works, a person verifies and decides. Yours to weigh.

Where does that leave you? AI is fluent, not accountable — it models likely words, not truth. One answer: a human check on what matters — the bet behind augmented intelligence: AI works, a person verifies and decides. Yours to weigh.
recall-q: Why can't you just trust an AI answer because it sounds sure of itself?
Why can't you trust an AI answer just because it sounds confident?
Why can't you trust an AI answer just because it sounds confident?
Why does an AI's confidence not prove it's right?
Why doesn't AI confidence imply correctness?
recall-a: Because it puts together words that sound likely, not facts it has checked. So it says made-up things in the very same sure voice it uses for true ones. How confident it sounds tells you nothing about whether it's true — so check anything that matters.
Because it predicts likely words, not checked facts — so it states made-up things in the same confident voice as true ones. Confidence tells you nothing about truth; verify anything that matters.
It predicts plausible words, not verified facts — so it states made-up things in the same confident voice as true ones. Confidence tells you nothing about truth; verify anything that matters.
It predicts plausible words, not verified facts, voicing fabrications as confidently as truths. Confidence is no signal of truth; verify what matters.
It predicts plausible words, not verified facts; confidence is uncorrelated with truth. Verify what matters.
